past_medical_history: Focal epilepsy, diagnosed at age 58, well-controlled on levetiracetam with last seizure over 3 years ago
Major depressive disorder, diagnosed at age 60, treated with fluoxetine with good response
Hypertension, diagnosed at age 55, well-controlled on lisinopril
Chronic low back pain, managed conservatively with physical therapy
Mild bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, diagnosed 4 years ago, fitted with hearing aids

medications.frequency: as needed, up to 3 times daily

family_history: Mother had progressive cognitive decline in her 70s, died at age 82 with advanced dementia (type never formally diagnosed)
Father died at age 75 from myocardial infarction, no known neurological disease
Younger sister, age 62, with well-controlled epilepsy since adolescence
Maternal aunt had visual hallucinations and parkinsonism in her late 60s, diagnosed with Lewy body dementia
No known family history of stroke or multiple sclerosis

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.occupation: Retired high school mathematics teacher, retired 2 years ago due to increasing difficulty with lesson planning
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a two-story house, husband reports increasing concern about her safety on stairs
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 3 miles daily, now limited to short walks due to spatial disorientation

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place. Oriented to month and year but not exact date. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 23/30 with points lost on visuospatial/executive (clock drawing incomplete, cube copy poorly reproduced), naming (missed 1), and delayed recall (3/5 words recalled after cue). Able to spell WORLD backward. Language fluency grossly intact with occasional word-finding pauses. Able to follow 3-step commands. Reading aloud intact but reports difficulty comprehending written paragraphs. Serial 7s performed slowly but accurately.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual acuity 20/25 bilaterally with correction. Visual field testing by confrontation reveals subtle difficulty in bilateral lower quadrants — patient hesitant and slow to detect finger movements, though formal perimetry not performed. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing reduced bilaterally consistent with known sensorineural loss, improved with hearing aids. Palate elevates symmetrically. Shoulder shrug strong bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. Normal bulk and tone. No tremor at rest or with action.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. Graphesthesia impaired bilaterally in hands — unable to correctly identify numbers drawn on palms in 4 of 6 trials. Stereognosis mildly impaired in both hands — hesitant identification of common objects.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with normal stride length and arm swing. Romberg negative. Tandem gait performed slowly but without falling. Patient occasionally misjudges doorway widths and bumps into doorframes during clinic visit.
neurological_exam.additional: Simultanagnosia suggested by inability to describe the overall scene in the Boston Cookie Theft picture — identifies individual elements (woman, sink, cookies) but cannot integrate them into a coherent narrative. Optic ataxia suspected — misreaches when attempting to grab a pen held in peripheral visual field. Oculomotor apraxia not formally tested but patient exhibits difficulty shifting gaze smoothly between examiner's fingers in bilateral visual fields. No ideomotor apraxia. No limb-kinetic apraxia. No alien limb phenomenon.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty reading, navigating familiar routes, and judging distances over the past 18 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Elira Hoxha is a 66-year-old right-handed Albanian-American woman, a retired high school mathematics teacher, who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic for evaluation of progressive visuospatial difficulties and functional decline over approximately 18 months. Her husband first noticed changes roughly two years ago when she began having trouble parallel parking, a task she had always performed effortlessly. Around the same time, she started misplacing items not because she forgot where she put them, but because she seemed unable to visually locate objects sitting in plain view on the kitchen counter. Over the following months, she developed increasing difficulty reading, initially attributed to her known mild cataracts and hearing-related fatigue, but an ophthalmology evaluation confirmed stable visual acuity at 20/25 bilaterally with no explanation for her reading difficulties. She describes the text as appearing to 'jump around' and reports being unable to follow lines across a page. She has become reliant on audiobooks. Her husband reports that she has become disoriented while driving in their neighborhood of 30 years, missing turns and occasionally driving past their house. Six months ago, she had a minor fender-bender when she misjudged the distance to a parked car, and the family decided she should stop driving. She has also had two falls at home, both attributed to misjudging the position of stair edges. Importantly, her episodic memory has remained relatively intact — she accurately recalls conversations from days prior, remembers family birthdays, and follows current events through television news. Her husband notes she is emotionally more irritable and anxious than her baseline, particularly when navigating unfamiliar environments, but there have been no personality changes, no behavioral disinhibition, and no hallucinations. Her speech remains fluent and her word-finding is only mildly affected. The patient has a history of focal epilepsy diagnosed at age 58, which has been well-controlled on levetiracetam with no seizures in over 3 years. She also has a history of major depressive disorder treated with fluoxetine. Her neurologist referred her for further evaluation after noting the visuospatial complaints could not be explained by her seizure disorder or depression. There is concern about a neurodegenerative process given the progressive nature and functional impact, though the atypical presentation with preserved memory has made the referring physician uncertain about the diagnosis.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythm: Low-amplitude beta activity, no frontal intermittent rhythmic delta

findings.location: Bilateral posterior regions (O1, O2, P3, P4)
findings.frequency: 5-6 Hz theta activity, intermittent runs of 2-4 seconds

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age and cooperation concerns
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 8-14 Hz frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Intermittent posterior theta slowing of uncertain significance — may reflect early posterior cortical dysfunction or normal variant. No electrographic seizures. No diffuse slowing to suggest encephalopathy. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Awake recording only; sleep stages not captured. Extended monitoring or sleep-deprived EEG may provide additional information.

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital regions, more prominent on the right

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent sulcal widening in bilateral parieto-occipital cortex with relative preservation of medial temporal structures
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No periventricular or deep white matter signal abnormality beyond age-expected changes

findings.location: Periventricular and scattered deep white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Confluent periventricular hyperintensities with scattered punctate deep white matter lesions

findings.borders: No restricted diffusion on DWI to suggest acute ischemia

findings.location: Posterior horns of lateral ventricles (colpocephaly pattern)
findings.size: Posterior horn width mildly increased relative to anterior horns

signal_characteristics.T1: CSF signal in mildly dilated posterior ventricular horns, suggesting ex vacuo enlargement from posterior cortical volume loss

findings.size: Scheltens medial temporal atrophy score: Grade 1 bilaterally

signal_characteristics.T1_coronal: Mild widening of choroidal fissure bilaterally. Hippocampal height preserved. No significant hippocampal volume loss beyond age-expected range.

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Within normal limits for age (25th percentile bilaterally on NeuroQuant)
volumetrics.parietal_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally, right more than left (below 5th percentile for age)
volumetrics.frontal_cortical_thickness: Within normal limits for age (40th percentile)
volumetrics.temporal_cortical_thickness: Within normal limits for age (35th percentile)

additional_observations: No mass lesions or abnormal enhancement on post-gadolinium sequences
MRA of the circle of Willis is unremarkable — no aneurysms, stenoses, or occlusions
No evidence of cerebral amyloid angiopathy-related microbleeds on SWI
Mild mucosal thickening in the left ethmoid sinus — incidental
mri.impression: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortical atrophy with relative sparing of medial temporal structures. Moderate white matter hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 2) may represent chronic small vessel disease, which could contribute to or confound the clinical picture. Hippocampal volumes are within normal limits for age. No acute pathology identified. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
Occasional premature atrial complexes (2 noted in rhythm strip)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with rare premature atrial complexes. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia to suggest cardiac etiology for cognitive symptoms.

findings.test: Visual Object and Space Perception Battery (VOSP) - Dot Counting

output.impression: Abnormal values: MoCA 23/30 score (H), Rey Complex Figure Copy 18/36 score (H), Rey Complex Figure Delayed Recall 8/36 score (H), Trail Making Test A 68 seconds seconds (H), Trail Making Test B 142 seconds seconds (H), Judgment of Line Orientation 14/30 score (H), Visual Object and Space Perception Battery (VOSP) - Dot Counting 5/10 score (H).

findings.signal: cortical_SUVr: Globally elevated cortical-to-cerebellar SUVr of 1.42 (positive threshold >1.11); regional_pattern: Highest uptake in bilateral parietal and occipital cortices (SUVr 1.58), followed by lateral temporal (SUVr 1.38) and frontal cortices (SUVr 1.31). Posterior-predominant distribution.; cerebellar_reference: Normal cerebellar uptake used as reference region

output.impression: Positive amyloid PET scan demonstrating elevated cortical amyloid-beta deposition with a posterior-predominant distribution. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.signal: hypometabolic_regions: Marked bilateral parieto-occipital hypometabolism, right greater than left, with extension into the posterior temporal-parietal junction; preserved_regions: Relative preservation of frontal cortex, anterior temporal cortex, and medial temporal structures including hippocampi; cerebellar_activity: Normal cerebellar metabolism; cingulate: Posterior cingulate cortex shows mild hypometabolism; anterior cingulate preserved

output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.neurofilament_light: 22 pg/mL (mildly elevated for age; reference <15 pg/mL)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/uL (normal: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL (normal: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 62 mg/dL (normal: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.65 (normal: >0.6)). Abeta42: 385 pg/mL (abnormal: <500 pg/mL suggests amyloid pathology). Abeta40: 8200 pg/mL (normal reference). Abeta42_40_ratio: 0.047 (abnormal: <0.062 suggests amyloid pathology). phospho_tau_181: 68 pg/mL (abnormal: >60 pg/mL suggests AD pathology). total_tau: 445 pg/mL (borderline: >400 pg/mL suggests neurodegeneration). neurofilament_light: 22 pg/mL (mildly elevated for age; reference <15 pg/mL). 14_3_3_protein: Negative. oligoclonal_bands: None detected. cytology: No malignant cells.

abnormal_values_summary: APOE e3/e4: heterozygous e4 carrier (increased AD risk) (H)

output.query: posterior cortical atrophy diagnostic criteria Alzheimer disease

results.title: Consensus classification of posterior cortical atrophy
results.summary: International consensus criteria for PCA. Core features: insidious onset, gradual progression, prominent early visuospatial/visuoperceptual deficits with relative preservation of memory and language. Supportive features include simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia (Balint syndrome), environmental agnosia, dressing apraxia, and alexia.

results.title: Posterior cortical atrophy: a review of neuropsychological and neuroimaging findings
results.summary: PCA accounts for approximately 5% of AD cases. Most common underlying pathology is AD (>80%), but can also be caused by LBD, corticobasal degeneration, or prion disease. Key distinguishing feature from typical AD is preserved episodic memory with prominent visuospatial deficits.

results.title: Amyloid and tau PET in posterior cortical atrophy
results.summary: Amyloid PET positive in >90% of PCA patients. Tau PET shows posterior-predominant uptake pattern correlating with clinical severity. Recommends amyloid PET as confirmatory test when clinical suspicion for PCA exists.

output.summary: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is a neurodegenerative syndrome characterized by progressive visuospatial and visuoperceptual deficits with relative preservation of episodic memory. The most common underlying pathology is Alzheimer's disease (>80% of cases). Diagnostic criteria require insidious onset, gradual progression, and prominent early visual/spatial deficits disproportionate to memory impairment. Amyloid PET is positive in >90% of PCA cases and is recommended for diagnostic confirmation. Key differential diagnoses include Lewy body dementia, corticobasal degeneration, and prion disease.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg once daily for Alzheimer's disease
interactions: Donepezil + Fluoxetine: Moderate interaction — fluoxetine (CYP2D6 inhibitor) may increase donepezil levels by 20-30%. Monitor for cholinergic side effects (nausea, diarrhea, bradycardia). Consider dose adjustment if symptoms occur.
Donepezil + Levetiracetam: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both may lower seizure threshold in theory but levetiracetam provides protective coverage.
warnings: Monitor for GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) which are common with cholinesterase inhibitors
Monitor heart rate — donepezil may cause bradycardia, particularly with concomitant medications that affect cardiac conduction
Cholinesterase inhibitors may theoretically lower seizure threshold — monitor closely given patient's epilepsy history
output.formulary_status: Covered under most insurance plans; generic available
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch (transdermal, fewer GI side effects)
Memantine (NMDA receptor antagonist, different mechanism; can be added later for combination therapy)

output.query: progressive myoclonic epilepsy cognitive decline differential diagnosis Alzheimer disease

results.title: Progressive myoclonic epilepsies: a comprehensive review
results.summary: Progressive myoclonic epilepsies (PMEs) are characterized by myoclonus, seizures, and progressive neurological decline. Unlike AD, PMEs typically present in adolescence or young adulthood. Lafora disease, Unverricht-Lundborg disease, NCL, MERRF, and sialidosis are the main PMEs. Cognitive decline in PME is more rapid and associated with prominent myoclonus and seizure progression.

results.title: Distinguishing neurodegenerative dementias from progressive myoclonic epilepsies
results.summary: Key differentiating features: PMEs present with prominent myoclonus and seizure worsening; age of onset is typically <30. Late-onset epilepsy with cognitive decline should raise suspicion for neurodegenerative dementia (AD, DLB, FTD) rather than PME. Overlap may occur in rare genetic forms.

output.summary: Progressive myoclonic epilepsies (PMEs) and Alzheimer's disease both feature cognitive decline, but they differ markedly in age of onset, progression pattern, and clinical features. PMEs typically present before age 30 with prominent myoclonus and progressive seizures. Late-onset epilepsy with cognitive decline is more suggestive of neurodegenerative dementia. The patient's age of onset (mid-60s), visuospatial-predominant cognitive decline, and well-controlled epilepsy are inconsistent with a PME and strongly favor a neurodegenerative etiology.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (visual variant of early-stage Alzheimer's disease)

differential.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease
differential.key_distinguishing: Progressive visuospatial deficits with relatively preserved episodic memory, posterior-predominant cortical atrophy on MRI, features of Balint syndrome (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia). Confirmed by positive amyloid PET and AD-pattern CSF biomarkers.

Fazekas grade 2 white matter changes could contribute to cognitive deficits, but the progressive visuospatial-predominant pattern and positive amyloid biomarkers favor AD pathology as primary etiology. No stepwise decline or discrete vascular events.

Can present with visuospatial deficits and posterior cortical involvement, but patient lacks core features: no visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism, no REM sleep behavior disorder, no fluctuating cognition. Family history of aunt with LBD noted but insufficient to shift diagnosis.
Can cause asymmetric parietal atrophy and apraxia, but typically presents with asymmetric rigidity, alien limb phenomenon, and cortical sensory loss. Patient lacks motor findings.

Heidenhain variant of CJD can present with visual cortical symptoms, but progression is far more rapid (weeks to months), and negative CSF 14-3-3 protein and normal EEG (no periodic sharp wave complexes) argue strongly against prion disease.

History of depression treated with fluoxetine, but depressive symptoms are below clinical threshold on GDS. Progressive visuospatial deficits over 18 months with structural brain changes are not explained by depression.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive history focusing on visuospatial symptoms, functional decline, and temporal progression
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive visuospatial deficits with preserved episodic memory over 18 months

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination including assessment for Balint syndrome components
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, impaired graphesthesia/stereognosis; preserved motor function and gait

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes and pattern of atrophy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy with spared medial temporal structures; white matter disease as confounder

optimal_actions.action: Order routine dementia workup labs (CBC, BMP, TSH, B12, folate, RPR, HIV)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All within normal limits, ruling out reversible metabolic causes

optimal_actions.action: Request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severely impaired visuospatial function with preserved episodic memory — PCA pattern

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET or CSF AD biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET with posterior-predominant deposition OR abnormal CSF Abeta42/tau profile

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET to evaluate regional metabolic patterns if diagnosis remains uncertain
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant hypometabolism with spared medial temporal structures

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on posterior cortical atrophy diagnostic criteria
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Consensus criteria for PCA supporting the clinical and biomarker diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate interaction with fluoxetine; monitoring needed; safe to proceed with donepezil

optimal_actions.action: Request APOE genotyping for risk stratification
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE e3/e4 heterozygosity — increased AD risk, supportive but not diagnostic

critical_actions: Recognize that visuospatial-predominant cognitive decline with preserved memory suggests posterior cortical atrophy, not typical amnestic AD
Order brain MRI to identify the posterior-predominant atrophy pattern
Order routine dementia screening labs to exclude reversible causes (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Request formal neuropsychological testing to document the dissociation between impaired visuospatial function and preserved memory
Obtain biomarker confirmation of Alzheimer pathology (amyloid PET or CSF Abeta42/tau)
Do not dismiss symptoms as age-related visual changes or attribute them solely to cataracts or epilepsy
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to the patient's known epilepsy without investigating neurodegenerative causes
Attributing visuospatial symptoms solely to ophthalmologic disease without neurological evaluation
Diagnosing typical amnestic Alzheimer's disease without recognizing the atypical PCA presentation
Starting anticholinergic medications, which would worsen cognitive function
Discontinuing levetiracetam without clear indication, potentially unmasking seizures
Prescribing antipsychotics for irritability without excluding Lewy body pathology
Performing lumbar puncture without brain imaging first in a patient with cognitive decline
key_reasoning_points: Progressive visuospatial deficits (reading difficulty, spatial disorientation, impaired object localization) with preserved episodic memory is the hallmark presentation of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA)
PCA is most commonly caused by Alzheimer's disease pathology (~80% of cases) but presents with visual/spatial rather than memory symptoms
Features of Balint syndrome (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia) on examination are characteristic of PCA and should prompt consideration of this diagnosis
Normal ophthalmologic examination with progressive 'visual' complaints should raise suspicion for cortical visual processing disorder rather than primary eye disease
MRI showing posterior-predominant cortical atrophy with relative hippocampal sparing distinguishes PCA from typical amnestic AD
White matter hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 2) are a significant confounder — vascular cognitive impairment must be considered but does not explain the posterior-predominant syndrome
The EEG is essentially normal, which is expected in early PCA and argues against rapidly progressive conditions like CJD
History of well-controlled epilepsy is a red herring — the current progressive decline is unrelated to the seizure disorder
Fluoxetine for depression is another potential confounder, but depression screening scores are below threshold and the structural brain changes cannot be explained by mood disorder
Family history of maternal aunt with Lewy body dementia and mother with unspecified dementia adds complexity but should not override the clinical and biomarker evidence for AD-PCA
Positive amyloid PET or abnormal CSF Abeta42/tau profile provides biomarker confirmation of underlying AD pathology in this atypical presentation

metadata.condition_name: Early-stage Alzheimer's disease (posterior cortical atrophy variant)
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to atypical presentation: visuospatial-predominant cognitive decline with preserved episodic memory (posterior cortical atrophy) rather than classic amnestic AD. Multiple confounders include pre-existing epilepsy, depression on fluoxetine, significant white matter disease on MRI suggesting vascular etiology, family history pointing toward Lewy body dementia, and initially normal EEG. The critical diagnostic clue — posterior-predominant cortical atrophy pattern — requires integration with neuropsychological testing and biomarker confirmation.
